html:
XX

Novikoff, when he opened the door himself to Sanine, looked far from pleased at the prospect of such a visit. Everything that reminded him of Lida and of his shattered dream of bliss caused him pain.

Sanine noticed this, and came into the room smiling affably. All there was in disorder, as if scattered by a whirlwind. Scraps of paper, straw, and rubbish of all sorts covered the floor. On the bed and the chairs lay books, linen, surgical instruments and a portmanteau.

тАЬGoing away?тАЭ asked Sanine, in surprise. тАЬWhere?тАЭ Novikoff avoided the otherтАЩs glance and continued to overhaul the things, vexed at his own confusion. At last he said:

тАЬYes, IтАЩve got to leave this place. IтАЩve had my official notice.тАЭ

Sanine looked at him and then at the portmanteau. After another glance his features relaxed in a broad smile.

Novikoff was silent, oppressed by his sense of utter loneliness and his inconsolable grief. Lost in his thoughts, he proceeded to wrap up a pair of boots together with some glass tubes.

тАЬIf you pack like that,тАЭ said Sanine, тАЬwhen you arrive youтАЩll find yourself minus either tubes or boots.тАЭ

NovikoffтАЩs tear-stained eyes flashed back a reply. They said, тАЬAh! leave me alone! Surely you can see how sad I am!тАЭ

Sanine understood, and was silent.

The dreamy summer twilight-hour had come, and above the verdant garden the sky, clear as crystal, grew paler. At last Sanine spoke.

тАЬInstead of going the deuce knows where, I think it would be much more sensible if you were to marry Lida.тАЭ

Novikoff turned round trembling.

тАЬI must ask you to stop making such stupid jokes!тАЭ he said in a shrill, hard voice. It rang out through the dusk, and echoed among the dreaming garden-trees.

тАЬWhy so furious?тАЭ asked Sanine.

тАЬLook here!тАЭ began Novikoff hoarsely. In his eyes there was such an expression of rage that Sanine scarcely recognized him.

тАЬDo you mean to say that it wouldnтАЩt be a lucky thing for you to marry Lida?тАЭ continued Sanine merrily.

тАЬShut up!тАЭ cried the other, staggering forward, and brandishing an old boot over SanineтАЩs head.

тАЬNow then! Gently! Are you mad?тАЭ said Sanine sharply, as he stepped backwards.

Novikoff flung the boot away in disgust, breathing hard.

тАЬWith that boot you were actually going toтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ Sanine stopped, and shook his head. He pitied his friend, though such behaviour seemed to him utterly ridiculous.

тАЬItтАЩs your fault,тАЭ stammered Novikoff in confusion.

And then, suddenly, he felt full of trust and sympathy for Sanine, strong and calm as he was. He himself resembled a little schoolboy, eager to tell someone of his trouble. Tears filled his eyes.

тАЬIf you only knew how sad at heart I am,тАЭ he murmured, striving to conquer his emotion.

тАЬMy dear fellow, I know all about itтБатАФeverything,тАЭ said Sanine kindly.

тАЬNo! You canтАЩt know all!тАЭ said Novikoff, as he sat down beside the other. He thought that no one could possibly feel such sorrow as his.

тАЬYes, yes, I do,тАЭ replied Sanine, тАЬI swear that I do; and if youтАЩll promise not to attack me with your old boot, I will prove what I say. Promise?тАЭ

тАЬYes, yes! Forgive me, Volodja!тАЭ said Novikoff, calling Sanine by his first name which he had never done before. This touched Sanine, and he felt the more anxious to help his friend.

тАЬWell, then, listen,тАЭ he began, as he placed his hand in confidential fashion on the otherтАЩs knee. тАЬLet us be quite frank. You are going away, because Lida refused you, and because, at SarudineтАЩs the other day, you had an idea that it was she who came to see him in private.тАЭ

Novikoff bent forward, too distressed to speak. It was as if Sanine had reopened an agonizing wound. The latter, noticing NovikoffтАЩs agitation, thought inwardly, тАЬYou good-natured old fool!тАЭ

Then he continued:

тАЬAs to the relations between Lida and Sarudine, I can affirm nothing positively, for I know nothing, but I donтАЩt believe that.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ He did not finish the sentence when he saw how dark the otherтАЩs face became.

тАЬTheir intimacy,тАЭ he went on, тАЬis of such recent date that nothing serious can have happened, especially if one considers LidaтАЩs character. You, of course, know what she is.тАЭ

There rose up before Novikoff the image of Lida, as he had once known and loved her; of Lida, the proud, high-spirited girl, lustrous-eyed, and crowned with serene, consummate beauty as with a radiant aureole. He shut his eyes, and put faith in SanineтАЩs words.

тАЬWell, and if they really did flirt a bit, thatтАЩs over and ended now. After all, what is it to you if a girl like Lida, young and fancy-free, has had a little amusement of this sort? Without any great effort of memory I expect you could recall at least a dozen such flirtations of a far more dangerous kind, too.тАЭ

Novikoff glanced trustfully at Sanine, afraid to speak, lest the faint spark of hope within him should be extinguished. At last he stammered out:

тАЬYou know, if IтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ; but he got no further. Words failed him, and tears choked his utterance.

тАЬWell, if you what?тАЭ asked Sanine loudly, and his eyes shone. тАЬI can but tell you this, that there is not and there never has been anything between Lida and Sarudine.тАЭ

Novikoff looked at him in amazement.

тАЬIтБатАКтБатАж wellтБатАКтБатАж I thoughtтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ he began, feeling, to his dismay, that he could no longer believe what Sanine said.

тАЬYou thought a lot of nonsense!тАЭ replied Sanine sharply. тАЬYou ought to know Lida better than that. What sort of love can there be with all that hesitation and shilly-shallying?тАЭ

Novikoff, overjoyed, grasped the otherтАЩs hand.

Then, suddenly SanineтАЩs face wore a furious expression as he closely watched the effect of his words upon his companion.

Novikoff showed obvious pleasure at the thought of the woman he desired being immaculate. Into those honest sorrowful eyes, there came a look of animal jealousy and concupiscence.

тАЬOho!тАЭ exclaimed Sanine threateningly, as he got up. тАЬThen what I have to tell you is this: Lida has not only fallen in love with Sarudine, but she has also had illicit relations with him, and is now enceinte.тАЭ

There was dead silence in the room. Novikoff smiled a strange, sickly smile and rubbed his hands. From his trembling lips there issued a faint cry. Sanine stood over him, looking straight into his eyes. The wrinkled corners of his mouth showed suppressed anger.

тАЬWell, why donтАЩt you speak?тАЭ he asked.

Novikoff looked up for a moment, but instantly avoided the otherтАЩs glance, his features being still distorted by a vacuous smile.

тАЬLida has just gone through a terrible ordeal,тАЭ said Sanine in a low voice, as if soliloquising. тАЬIf I had not chanced to overtake her, she would not be living now, and what yesterday was a healthful, handsome girl would now be lying in the river-mud, a bloated corpse, devoured by crabs. The question is not one of her deathтБатАФwe must each of us die some dayтБатАФyet how sad to think that with her all the brightness and joy created for others by her personality would also have perished. Of course, Lida is not the only one in all the world; but, my God! if there were no girlish loveliness left, it would be as sad and gloomy as the grave.

тАЬFor my part, I am eager to commit murder when I see a poor girl brought to ruin in this senseless way. Personally, it is a matter of utter indifference to me whether you marry Lida or go to the devil, but I must tell you that you are an idiot. If you had got one sound idea in your head, would you worry yourself and others so much merely because a young woman, free to pick and choose, had become the mistress of a man who was unworthy of her, and by following her sexual impulse had achieved her own complete development? Nor are you the only idiot, let me tell you. There are millions of your sort who make life into a prison, without sunshine or warmth! How often have you given rein to your lust in company with some harlot, the sharer of your sordid debauch? In LidaтАЩs case it was passion, the poetry of youth, and strength, and beauty. By what right, then, do you shrink from her, you that call yourself an intelligent, sensible man? What has her past to do with you? Is she less beautiful? Or less fitted for loving, or for being loved? Is it that you yourself wanted to be the first to possess her? Now then, speak!тАЭ

тАЬYou know very well that it is not that!тАЭ said Novikoff, as his lips trembled.

тАЬAh! yes, but it is!тАЭ cried Sanine. тАЬWhat else could it be, pray?тАЭ

Novikoff was silent. All was darkness within his soul, yet, as a distant ray of light through the gloom there came the thought of pardon and self-sacrifice.

Sanine, watching him, seemed to read what was passing through his mind.

тАЬI see,тАЭ he began, in a subdued tone, тАЬthat you contemplate sacrificing yourself for her. тАШI will descend to her level, and protect her from the mob,тАЩ and so on. ThatтАЩs what you are saying to your virtuous self, waxing big in your own eyes as a worm does in carrion. But itтАЩs all a sham; nothing else but a lie! YouтАЩre not in the least capable of self-sacrifice. If, for instance, Lida had been disfigured by smallpox, perhaps you might have worked yourself up to such a deed of heroism. But after a couple of days you would have embittered her life, either spurning her or deserting her, or overwhelming her with reproaches. At present your attitude towards yourself is one of adoration, as if you were an icon. Yes, yes, your face is transfigured, and everyone would say, тАШOh! look, thereтАЩs a saint.тАЩ Yet you have lost nothing which you desired. LidaтАЩs limbs are the same as before; so are her passion and her splendid vitality. But of course, it is extremely convenient and also agreeable to provide oneself with enjoyment while piously imagining that one is doing a noble deed. I should rather say it was!тАЭ

At these words, NovikoffтАЩs self-pity gave place to a nobler sentiment.

тАЬYou take me to be worse than I am,тАЭ he said reproachfully. тАЬI am not so wanting in feeling as you think. I wonтАЩt deny that I have certain prejudices, but I love Lida Petrovna, and if I were quite sure that she loved me, do you think that I should take a long while to make up my mind, becauseтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

His voice failed him at this last word.

Sanine suddenly became quite calm. Crossing the room, he stood at the open window, lost in thought.

тАЬJust now she is very sad,тАЭ he said, тАЬand will hardly be thinking of love. If she loves you or not, how can I tell? But it seems to me that if you came to her as the second man who did not condemn her for her brief amour, well.тБатАКтБатАж Anyway, thereтАЩs no knowing what sheтАЩll say!тАЭ

Novikoff sat there, as one in a dream. Sadness and joy produced within his heart a sense of happiness as gentle and elusive as the light in an evening sky.

тАЬLet us go to her,тАЭ said Sanine. тАЬWhatever happens, it will please her to see a human face amid so many false masks that hide grimacing brutes. YouтАЩre a bit of a fool, my friend, but in your stupidity there is something which others havenтАЩt got. And to think that for ever so long the world founded its hopes and happiness upon such folly! Come, let us go!тАЭ

Novikoff smiled timidly. тАЬI am very willing to go to her. But will she care to see me?тАЭ

тАЬDonтАЩt think about that,тАЭ said Sanine, as he placed both hands on the otherтАЩs shoulders. тАЬIf you are minded to do whatтАЩs right, then, do it, and the future will take care of itself.тАЭ

тАЬAll right; let us go,тАЭ exclaimed Novikoff with decision. In the doorway he stopped and looking Sanine full in the face he said with unwonted emphasis:

тАЬLook here, if it is in my power, I will do my best to make her happy. This sounds commonplace, I know, but I canтАЩt express my feelings in any other way.тАЭ

тАЬNo matter, my friend,тАЭ replied Sanine cordially, тАЬI understand.тАЭ
